coredata.dc:title: Foliar application of a newly engineered cysteine-graphene quantum dots (GQDs-Cys) nanocomposite mitigates drought stress in apricot (Prunus armeniaca L.) seedlings

coredata.dc:description: Drought stress is one of the most important environmental constraints that negatively affects the growth and yield of crops worldwide. Recently, nanotechnology has been increasingly employed to improve the tolerance of plants exposed to abiotic stresses such as drought. The present study was designed to investigate the moderating effect of cysteine-graphene quantum dots (GQDs-Cys) on alleviating drought stress in apricot cv. ‘Askarabad’. The interaction between GQDs, Cys, and GQDs-Cys NPs at 10 mg l-1 and water stress (100, 50, and 25% of field capacity) was evaluated by measuring some physiological, chlorophyll fluorescence, and biochemical parameters. The treatments GQDs, Cys, and GQDs-Cys nanoparticles generally mitigated the detrimental effects of 50% and 25% water stress, greatly enhancing the relative water relation characteristics of apricot leaves. GQDs, Cys, and especially GQDs-Cys NPs significantly reduced chlorophyll damage under high drought levels. The presence of GQDs-Cys NPs declined the damage caused by drought in apricots, which was shown by lower levels of MDA and EL. Moreover, drought stress and foliar application of GQDs-Cys resulted in a substantial enhancement in GPX, APX, SOD, and PPO enzyme activities, as well as total flavonoids, phenolics content, and total antioxidant activity in comparison to control plants. The use of GQDs-Cys NPs in stressed and non-stressed conditions positively improved the studied traits of the apricot plants by enhancing the antioxidant system. Taken together, the present findings suggest that GQDs-Cys NPs can be used as promising drought stress-reducing agents in apricot seedlings. The present results indicate that amino acids, including cysteine-doped GQDs, could act as effective nanomaterials for enhancing the development of plants, particularly fruit trees and this article will serve as a reference as it is the very first report regarding the ameliorative role of GQDs-Cys in drought stress mitigation.

head.ce:title: Foliar application of a newly engineered cysteine-graphene quantum dots (GQDs-Cys) nanocomposite mitigates drought stress in apricot (Prunus armeniaca L.) seedlings

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Drought stress is one of the most important environmental constraints that negatively affects the growth and yield of crops worldwide. Recently, nanotechnology has been increasingly employed to improve the tolerance of plants exposed to abiotic stresses such as drought. The present study was designed to investigate the moderating effect of cysteine-graphene quantum dots (GQDs-Cys) on alleviating drought stress in apricot cv. ‘Askarabad’. The interaction between GQDs, Cys, and GQDs-Cys NPs at 10 mg l-1 and water stress (100, 50, and 25% of field capacity) was evaluated by measuring some physiological, chlorophyll fluorescence, and biochemical parameters. The treatments GQDs, Cys, and GQDs-Cys nanoparticles generally mitigated the detrimental effects of 50% and 25% water stress, greatly enhancing the relative water relation characteristics of apricot leaves. GQDs, Cys, and especially GQDs-Cys NPs significantly reduced chlorophyll damage under high drought levels. The presence of GQDs-Cys NPs declined the damage caused by drought in apricots, which was shown by lower levels of MDA and EL. Moreover, drought stress and foliar application of GQDs-Cys resulted in a substantial enhancement in GPX, APX, SOD, and PPO enzyme activities, as well as total flavonoids, phenolics content, and total antioxidant activity in comparison to control plants. The use of GQDs-Cys NPs in stressed and non-stressed conditions positively improved the studied traits of the apricot plants by enhancing the antioxidant system. Taken together, the present findings suggest that GQDs-Cys NPs can be used as promising drought stress-reducing agents in apricot seedlings. The present results indicate that amino acids, including cysteine-doped GQDs, could act as effective nanomaterials for enhancing the development of plants, particularly fruit trees and this article will serve as a reference as it is the very first report regarding the ameliorative role of GQDs-Cys in drought stress mitigation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Plants are frequently subjected to environmental conditions, embracing biotic and abiotic stressors (Mansoor et al., 2021). Abiotic stresses create a significant hazard to global food security due to a continuously growing human population (Yadav et al., 2021). Drought is a universal abiotic stress that limits crop quantity and quality globally. With the greenhouse effect causing climate change, droughts are becoming more severe and frequent (Hong et al., 2020). Drought disrupts vital processes in plants such as photosynthesis, respiration, and ion transport, ultimately reducing crop productivity. Therefore, it is crucial to develop strategies to mitigate the adverse effects of drought on agriculture (Anjum et al., 2017). Drought stress triggers the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which consist of free radicals such as hydroxyl, alkoxy, and superoxide, and non-radicals such as hydrogen peroxide and oxygen (Ibrahim et al., 2019). These reactive compounds can damage proteins, carbohydrates, lipids, and DNA, disrupting cellular homeostasis. This can lead to negative effects on plant growth, such as stunted height, reduced canopy and root development, and decreased leaf area index (Yadav et al., 2021). As a result of drought, plants experience decreased leaf growth, cellular dehydration, oxidative damage, disturbed enzyme activity, and reduced photosynthesis (Narayan et al., 2017). Excessive deposit of ROS impairs membrane stability and cellular function by disrupting processes such as assimilation, mineral uptake, and photosynthesis (Ahanger et al., 2019). Plants enhance their antioxidant pool and accumulate osmolytes to deactivate redundant ROS and protect metabolism (Begum et al., 2019). Antioxidants consist of enzymatic and non-enzymatic components that are present in all cellular compartments. They help stabilize cells by eliminating excess ROS (Ahmad et al., 2019). Plants have advanced various mechanisms to withstand drought stress, including morpho-physiological, metabolic, and molecular adaptations (Ali et al., 2020). A substantial number of research have investigated the drought tolerance of apricot trees from a physiological perspective. Changes in hormone levels, enhanced activity of associated oxidases or peroxidases, and elevated osmoregulatory compounds together bolster the resilience of apricot trees, similar to findings seen in other fruit plants (Jing et al., 2023). Water stress significantly reduced the relative water content of the leaf and the gas exchange of leaf stomata, whereas the levels of MDA and proline increased under stress conditions (Bakır et al., 2022).

Advanced nanotechnology provides several benefits, including the improved efficiency of chemicals in plant priming treatments. Nanoparticles (NPs) also retain the photosynthetic system, which is susceptible to stresses, through reducing osmotic and oxidative stress.Their application holds great promise for the agronomic industry (Ioannou et al., 2020). Nanoparticles (NPs) can greatly impact plant growth and development, in addition to the plant's ability to tolerate abiotic stresses, by effectively detoxifying ROS (Rico et al., 2015).

GQDs NPs have lower toxicity and better biocompatibility, making them a promising solution for plant growth and food security worldwide (Xu et al., 2020). It has been reported that the graphene application positively affects the growth and development of various plants, particularly under abiotic stress conditions. The application of amino acid-functionalized graphene has been found to increase chlorophyll fluorescence parameters, the photosynthetic pigments, proline, membrane stability index, antioxidant enzyme activities, phenols, and main ingredients of essential oils under normal and stressful circumstances (Chen et al., 2018; Ganjavi et al., 2021; Li et al., 2018). Carbon-based nanoparticles have been shown to mitigate the detrimental effects induced by abiotic stressors (Khan et al., 2017). A recent study indicates that GQD has relatively low or negligible toxicity toward biological materials, demonstrates sufficient biocompatibility, and allows for facile functionalization with other biomolecules and chemical entities (Kuo et al., 2017). Moreover, the significant hydrophilicity and notable cell permeability of this material render it suitable for water-based applications in biological systems (Li et al., 2018). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess a single atomic layer planar conjugated structure, extensive surface area, and oxygen-containing functional groups that provide active binding sites for drug loading and the transport of various compounds (Chen et al., 2018). The doping of GQDs with particular compounds, heteroatoms, nanomaterials, DNA strands, and enzymes has been documented (Facure et al., 2021). Heteroatoms incorporated into carbon-based nanomaterials such as GQDs may efficiently modulate their intrinsic properties, including surface and local chemical attributes (Liu et al., 2011). Nitrogen-doped graphene may effectively adjust the band gap of the host molecule (GQDs) to impart novel characteristics (Li et al., 2012).

Cysteine (Cys) as an amino acid contains thiol and functions similarly as an antioxidant, efficiently scavenging ROS (Elkelish et al., 2021). Furthermore, cysteine is involved in the synthesis of various essential and defensive compounds, for instance, phytoalexins, proteins, glucosinolates, glutathione, metallothioneins, thionins, and phytochelatins (Terzi and Yıldız, 2021). Cys is involved in various pathways of sulfur metabolism as well as in the production of methionine (Genisel et al., 2015). Several scientific studies have underscored the potential of exogenous Cys to enhance plant health significantly. This potential is further amplified by the thiol side chain's ability to undergo smooth oxidation, providing a protective shield against oxidative damage caused by biotic and abiotic stressors (Terzi and Yıldız, 2021). Cysteine is crucial in cellular responses to alterations in the redox environment, particularly concerning oxidative stress, and is significantly associated with pathological disorders including neurodegeneration (Gu and Robinson, 2016).

Apricot (Prunus armeniaca L.) is a fruit tree that belongs to the Rosaceae family. It is widely distributed in many countries around the world and is an important source of biochemicals. In addition, apricots are also valuable for the production of highly nutritious fruits (Harel-Beja et al., 2019). Moreover, apricots are commonly grown fruit trees all over the world (Elmenofy et al., 2021).

Graphene oxide, quantum dots, and Cys have individually demonstrated remarkable improvements in plant performance, particularly under abiotic and biotic stress conditions. Therefore, they are useful applicants for utilization in plants to mitigate the negative effects of stressors. Based on the positive effects of each of these materials, it is hypothesized that their cooperation in the nanostructure "GQDs-Cys" will result in better efficiency of Cys. We believe that GQDs, in conjunction with the amino acid cysteine, will serve as an effective elicitor to enhance plant development by alleviating the detrimental effects of drought stress. Subsequently, GQDs-Cys nanoparticles were synthesized and administered to Prunus armeniaca trees under both non-stress and water deficit stress conditions. Following the application of various nanomaterials, stress-related indices, morphological characteristics of seedlings, biochemical, enzymatic, and non-enzymatic antioxidant properties, as well as oxidative stress parameters, were assessed to determine the efficacy of the applied formulations. This is likely the first research investigating the impact of GQDs-Cys in alleviating drought stress in Prunus species.

This experiment was conducted in the research greenhouse of the Faculty of Agriculture, University of Maragheh, Iran. The homogeneous two-year-old seedlings of Prunus armeniaca L. cv. ‘Askarabad’ was provided by the nursery of the Horticultural Science Department, University of Maragheh Iran, by the relevant institutional and national guidelines and legislation. The seedlings were planted in 7-liter plastic pots filled with a 2:1 mixture of soil and organic manure, whose features are presented in Table 1 . The experiments were conducted in a completely randomized design, including three repetitions as a factorial. Experimental treatments comprised drought stress at three levels (100, 50, and 25 FC); the second factor contained foliar spraying of GQDs, Cys, and GQDs-Cys NPs at 10 mg l-1 and distilled water as a control. Apricot seedlings were exposed to different levels of water deficit (100%, 50%, and 25% of field capacity) after being transported to a greenhouse on June 23rd to simulate the water deficit conditions experienced in the Maragheh climate. Seven days before the water deficit and twice after the drought stress, with a seven-day interval, foliar application of 60 mL of GQDs, Cyc, and GQD-Cys NPs, and distilled water were applied. All spraying was done until the upper leaf surfaces were thoroughly wet. Young, fully-developed leaves were used to conduct physiological and biochemical analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cysteine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs-Cys)
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, 2.3 g of citric acid was combined with 50 mL of water, stirring the mixture for 30 min at ambient temperature. Afterward, ultrasound waves were used to sonicate the solution for another 30 min. As a result, a clear solution was formed. Subsequently, the solution pH was modified to 10 using potassium hydroxide. Next, the solution was carefully transferred to a 100-ml Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, sealed, and heated to 160 °C in an oven for 4 h. Once cooled to room temperature, l-cysteine was gradually introduced to the yellow solution while being constantly stirred for 30 min. The resulting mixture was then subjected to reflux at 80 °C for 24 h, ultimately producing a light yellow liquid of GQDs-CysNPs (Tam et al., 2015).

A Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis was performed to study the composition of Cys-GQDs NPs and detect any functional groups on their surface. The outcomes of this analysis are illustrated in Fig. 1 a. The solid, wide absorption band observed at 3445 cm-1 signifies the broadening vibration of OH groups, whereas the peak at around 3256 cm-1 indicates NH stretches. Then it was confirmed the existence of N atoms on the Cys-GQDs NPs surface. (Milenković et al., 2021). The band of aromatic C = C skeletal vibration located near 1630 cm-1 indicates the presence of a graphite structure (Xia et al., 2017). The band at about 1400 cm-1 originates from the stretching vibrations of CN, further demonstrating the successful doping of N elements into the graphene structure (Xia et al., 2017). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique measured the average hydrodynamic size of synthesized Cys-GQDs NPs as about 15 nm (Fig. 1b), and Fig. 2 represents the HR-TEM image of fabricated GQD-Cys nanoparticles.

The leaves' fresh weight (LFW) was evaluated immediately after plant harvest. The shoots were then dehydrated in an oven at 75 °C for 48 h, after which their dry weight (LDW) was quantified using a digital scale (A&D weighing, Japan, accuracy 0.01 mg). The height of the plants was measured using a digital caliper (Rasouli et al., 2023).

The method developed by Arnon (1949) was used in order to determine the levels of photosynthetic pigments chlorophyll a (Chl a), chlorophyll b (Chl b), and carotenoids (CARs) in the leaves. During this procedure, 0.5 g of fresh leaves were centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for ten minutes after being extracted with 10 mL of 80% acetone. The absorbance was finally determined using a spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan) to measure the absorbance at the wavelengths of 663, 645, and 470 nm, respectively. In order to determine the concentrations of Chl a, Chl b, and CARs, the following formulas were used throughout the process:

To determine the relative water content (RWC), leaf segments (1 cm²) were weighed (fresh weight) and then immersed in petri dishes filled with deionized water. Following 24 h in darkness, the samples were soaked with water and then weighed (TW). The dry weight of the components was documented after desiccation at 70 °C for 48 h (DW). The quantity of RWC was determined using the following formula developed by Hayat et al. (2007).
RWC =
[
FW − DW
TW − DW
]
×
100

To quantify proline concentration based on Bates et al. (1973); 0.5 g of fresh leaf samples were extracted with 10 ml of 3% sulfosalicylic acid in an ice bath to inhibit proline breakdown. The extracts were then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min at 4 °C. Subsequently, 2 ml of the supernatant was extracted and combined in glass tubes with 2 ml of ninhydrin acid and 2 ml of glacial acetic acid, then heated in a hot water bath before being promptly moved to an ice bath for one hour. Subsequently, 4 ml of toluene was incorporated into the mixture and vortexed for 20 s. The absorbance was measured using a spectrophotometer at 520 nm.

MDA content was determined by the methodology proposed by (Heath and Packer, 1968). A sample of 0.5 gs of leaf was homogenized by adding 5 mL of trichloroacetic acid (TCA) at a concentration of 0.1%. Following centrifugation at a speed of 10,000 rpm at a temperature of 4 °C for fifteen minutes, the supernatant, which was 300 mL in volume, was combined with one milliliter of thiobarbituric acid (TBA) at a concentration of 0.5%. The combination was then heated at 95 °C for thirty minutes. Finally, the reaction solution was cooled on ice in order to halt the reaction. Following centrifugation at a speed of 10,000 rpm for ten minutes, the absorbance was measured at wavelengths of 440, 532, and 600 nanometers.

The approach developed by Alexieva et al. (2001) was used to quantify hydrogen peroxide content. In a nutshell, 50 mg of fresh young leaves were digested with 5 mL of 0.001% TCA. The homogenous mixture was centrifuged at 4 °C and 12,000 rpm for 15 min. Following the centrifugation, 500 mL of the supernatant were combined with 500 mL of potassium phosphate buffer (100 mM) and 2 mL of potassium iodide (KI) solution. After that, the reaction mixture was allowed to incubate for 60 min at room temperature and in complete darkness. The absorbance was measured at 390 nm using a UV-1800 instrument manufactured by Shimadzu in Tokyo, Japan.

To determine EL, mature leaf discs of apricots were washed, incubated, and measured for electrical conductivity. The first conductivity (EC1) was recorded, followed by incubation in a water bath for the final conductivity (EC2) measurement. EL was calculated using a formula. An electrical conductivity meter (Hanna Instruments, Woonsocket, USA) was employed (Mosa et al., 2018).

( % )
=
(
E C 1 / E C 2
)
×
100

In order to determine the MSI, 200 mg of leaf samples were immersed in ten cubic centimeters of deionized water and heated in a water bath at a temperature of 40 °C for 30 min. Furthermore, the electrical conductivity (EC1) was determined. The EC2 of the second group of samples was determined after they were heated in a water bath at a temperature of 100 °C for 10 min. Later, the percentage of MSI was calculated using the method provided by Sairam (Sairam et al., 2002).
M S I =

1 −

E C 1 / E C 2
)

You will need to crush 0.2 g of the sample and then put it into 10 mL of ethanol to calculate the amount of total soluble carbohydrates in the leaf samples. For one hour, bring the mixture to a temperature of 8 °C. Mix one milliliter of extract, 0.5 mL of phenol, and 5 mL of 98% sulfuric acid. At a wavelength of 483 nm, a spectrophotometer was used to determine the total amount of soluble carbohydrates (Chen et al., 1993).

The TFC content was determined by combining 50 microliters of methanolic extract with 150 µL of sodium nitrite at a concentration of five percent for 5 min. Immediately, 300 µm of aluminum chloride with a concentration of 10% was added and allowed to remain at room temperature for 10 min. After that, to get the total volume up to 5 mL, 1 mL of NaOH at a concentration of 1 M was added. TFC content was determined by spectrophotometric analysis at 360 nm, and the results were represented as milligrams of quercetin equivalent per gram of fresh weight (Chang et al., 2002).

TPC was determined following the method described by Xu et al. (2010) by employing the Folin-Ciocalteu reagent. A 0.1 g leaf material was extracted using 5 mL ethanol. Following the addition of 0.5 mL of 50% Folin-Ciocalteu solution and 1 mL of 5% sodium bicarbonate, the solution was left to react for one hour. Subsequently, the resulting absorbance was measured using a spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 725 nm. The results were stated as mg gallic acid (GAE) per g FW.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant enzymes activity assays (ascorbate peroxidase (APX), superoxide dismutase (SOD), guaiacol peroxidase (GPX), and polyphenol oxidase (PPO), and total soluble proteins
ce:section.ce:para: After adding three milliliters of potassium phosphate buffer with a concentration of 50 µL, 0.5 g of leaf fresh weight with a pH of seven was vortexed to create a plant extract for the activity of enzymes and the protein content. In addition, the extract included 1 mM of Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and 1% of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP; weight-to-volume ratio). The extract was centrifuged for 15 min at a speed of 13,000 rpm. In order to determine the amount of protein and the enzyme activity, the supernatant was used. Cakmak et al. (2001) approach was employed to determine the level of activity of GPX enzyme. It was composed of 750 mL of potassium phosphate buffer with a concentration of 100 mM and a pH of 7, 100 mL of water with a concentration of 70 mM, 750 mL of guaiacol with a concentration of 10 mM, and the extract. A measurement of the GPX enzyme activity was taken at 470 nm. In order to determine the level of enzyme activity, the extinction coefficient, which is comparable to 26.6 mM-1 cm-1, was taken into consideration.

The approach developed by Chen and Asada Chen and Asada (1992) was used in order to examine the activity of APX. The reaction mixture included the leaf extract, 200 ml of ascorbate with a concentration of 2 mM, 750 ml of potassium phosphate buffer with a concentration of 100 mM, and 200 ml of hydrogen peroxide with a concentration of 2 mM. To initiate the reaction, hydrogen peroxide was added to the combination. The activity of the APX enzyme was evaluated in the absorbance at 290 nm using a spectrophotometer. In the process of calculating the activity of the APX enzyme, the extinction coefficient of 2.8 mM-1 cm-1 was also taken into consideration.

SOD activity was measured using a modified version of the technique that Fu and Huang (2001) had developed. So,63 mM of nitro tetrazolium blue (NBT), 1.3 mM of riboflavin, 13 mM of methionine, and 0.1 mM of EDTA were combined in a 50 mM phosphate buffer at a pH of 7.8 and 20 µL of the enzyme extract was added to the mixture. The absorbance of each reaction mixture was measured using a spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 560 nm after the test tubes holding the mixtures were exposed to light for 10 min.

The total protein content was quantified using the Bradford method (Bradford, 1976), using bovine serum albumin to establish a standard curve with solutions containing 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, or 1 mg mL-1, to which 100 mL of Bradford reagent was added. To create the Bradford reagent, 50 mg of Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250 was dissolved in 50 mL of methanol and 100 mL of 85% (w/v) phosphoric acid, then combined with 850 mL of distilled water and filtered. Subsequently, 1000 mL of Bradford reagent was combined with 50 mL of the extract and incubated for 5 min at ambient temperature. Absorbance was measured at 595 nm using a spectrophotometer.

To determine the reduced ascorbate (AsA) level, the apricot fresh leaves were placed in a solution of 150 μM phosphate buffer (pH = 7.4) and 200 μL of distilled water. To this mixture, 200 μL of 3% FeCl3, 400 μL of 10% trichloroacetic acid, 400 μL of 4% bipyridyl, 400 μL of 44% phosphoric acid, were added. After incubating the mixture at 37 °C for 60 min, the absorbance was measured at 525 nm. For the total ascorbate assay, 100 μL of 10 mM dithiothreitol (DTT) was added to the above reaction mixture and measured against a standard AsA curve prepared in the same systematic manner as before. Lastly, the dehydroascorbate (DHA) content was determined by subtracting the reduced AsA from the total AsA (Law et al., 1983).

According to Benzie and Szeto (1999), 200 mL of acetate buffer, 20 mL TPTZ solution, 20 mL of FeCl3 solution and 20 mL of distilled water were used to prepare the reagent for the Ferric reducing antioxidant power (FRAP) assay.. After homogenizing fresh leaf apricot and adding 2 mL of methanol, the mixture was centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 10 min. Following, 200 μl of the supernatant was added to 2950 μl of FRAP reagent, and the mixture was allowed to remain in an ambient environment for 30 min. The spectrophotometer was used to determine the absorbance at a wavelength of 593 nm.

The statistical software MSTAT-C version 2.1 was applied to two-way ANOVA analysis. The study employed the Least Significant Difference (LSD) test to compare the means of the evaluated traits at both 1% and 5% probability levels. Pearson's correlation and heat map cluster analysis were depicted using R Studio ver. 14.2.1.

The present findings revealed that drought stress reduced the growth of apricot seedlings and the lowest growth was obtained in the highest water deficit, while the spraying of nanoparticles of Cys, GQQs, and Cys-GQD improved the growth features as shown in Table 1.

The present observations revealed that drought stress significantly reduced the LFW and LDW compared to control plants. However, the application of Cys, GQDs, and Cys-GQDs NPs significantly improved these parameters. The apricot seedlings exposed to 25% FC with no foliar application had the lowest LFW and LDW, decreasing by 103.85% and 93.35%, respectively, compared to control plants. The LFW increased by 36.5%, 24.18%, and 44.07%, and the LDW increased by 51.57%, 24.80%, and 43.04% in 100%, 50%, and 25% FC, respectively, with the application of Cys-GQD NPs compared to the apricot seedlings without any foliar treatment (Table 1).

The study's results indicate that drought stress lessened the levels of chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, and carotenoids. The highest content of chlorophyll a and b, as well as carotenoid, was observed when the apricot plants were sprayed with Cys-GQDs NPs and under no drought stress. In contrast, the lowest levels of these pigments were recorded under severe drought stress with no foliar treatment. Severe water deficit caused a significant lessening in chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, and carotenoid levels by 49.73%, 145.90%, and 165.68%, respectively, compared to control plants with no foliar spraying. The application of Cys-GQDs NPs via foliar spray at 100%, 50%, and 25% FC increased chlorophyll levels by 71.63%, 27.80%, and 29.92%, respectively, compared to plants without foliar treatment. Application of Cys-GQDs NPs resulted in significant improvements in chlorophyll b levels in apricot plants under different water stress conditions, with increases of 58.08%, 67.94%, and 48.74% observed in 100, 50, and 25% FC, respectively, compared to untreated plants. It was observed that the highest carotenoid content was achieved when Cys-GQDs NPs were applied to the leaves with 100% FC. The application of Cys-GQDs NPs to leaves with 100, 50, and 25% FC increased the carotenoid content by 21.93%, 76.13%, and 56.08%, respectively, compared to plants that were not given foliar treatment (Table 1).

According to these findings, the proline content increased in the seedlings of apricots that were subjected to drought stress (Table 2). Severe drought stress without foliar application resulted in a 3.86-fold enhancement in proline content as compared to control plants. Also, the foliar application of Cys, GQDs, and Cys-GQDs NPs enhanced proline content. Additionally, these results showed that applying Cys-GQDs NPs decreased proline content by 51.21% and 120.21% in 50 and 25% FC, respectively, when compared to plants without foliar spray (Fig. 3b).

Based on the results, the leaves of apricot showed a significant enhancement in MDA, H2O2 content, and EL under drought stress (Table 2). The increment in MDA, H2O2 content, and EL was by 69.78%, 120.78%, and 262.5% when the apricot plants were exposed to 25% FC and without foliar treatment, compared to the control. However, the utilization of Cys, GQDs, and Cys-GQDs NPs meaningfully declined MDA, H2O2 content, and EL under drought stress conditions. In comparison with non-treated plants under severe drought stress conditions, Cys-GQDs NPs treatment reduced MDA, H2O2, and EL by 24.17%, 42.49%, and 50.74%, respectively (Fig. 4 a and b; Fig 5 a).

Improving MSI is crucial for the proper functioning of plants. In the apricot seedlings, the use of Cys, GQDs, and Cys-GQDs NPs has been found to enhance MSI (Table 3 ). The maximum of MSI was observed when Cys-GQDs NPs were applied through foliar spraying on non-stressed plants, which led to an increase in MSI of 15.06% compared to the control. However, plants facing drought stress showed a reduction in MSI, so it was reduced by 49.94% in plants with 25% FC as compared to the control (Fig. 5b and c).

The total soluble carbohydrates content was significantly decreased due to drought stress. However, it improved effectively through the foliar application of Cys, GQDs, and Cys-GQDs NPs (Table 3). The apricot plants exposed to 25% FC without foliar application had the lowest value of total soluble carbohydrates, which showed a 185.53% decrease compared to the control plants. However, the use of Cys-GQDs NPs had a positive impact on total soluble carbohydrates. It improved the total soluble carbohydrates by 49.32% and 38.22% in 50% and 25% FC, respectively, compared to the control group (Fig. 6 a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total flavonoids (TFC) and total phenolic (TPC) content
ce:section.ce:para: Drought stress caused a significant enhancement in the TPC and TFC content, up to 39.57% and 134.28%, respectively, compared to the control plants. In apricot seedlings that received a foliar application of Cys, GQDs, and Cys-GQDs NPs, TPC and TFC in the leaves of apricots were significantly improved (Table 3). The current study found that the lowest TPC content was observed in the controls without foliar application and under normal watering conditions (100% FC). However, when Cys-GQDs NPs were applied under severe drought conditions (25% FC), the TPC content increased up to 10.98%. On the other hand, the highest TFC was obtained in apricot plants foliar sprayed with Cys-GQDs under severe drought stress (25% FC), which was 28.81% higher than the controls (Fig 6b and c).

Under drought stress conditions, the total soluble protein content in the leaves of apricot plants enhanced significantly (Table 4). The total soluble protein content increased by a staggering 104.41% when the FC level was below 25% without any foliar application. Under all three levels of drought stress, the total protein content increased in the presence of both Cys and GQDs and Cys-GQDs NPs. The application of Cys-GQDs NPs led to a meaningful increment in protein content. The protein content increased up to 55.6%, 32.35%, 31.88%, and 28.57% under 100%, 50%, and 25% FC, respectively (Fig. 7a).

In the current study found that Cys, GQDs, and Cys-GQD NPs significantly impacted the non-enzymatic antioxidants (Table 5 ). At 25% of FC, the highest AsA content was revealed in plants without foliar application by 52.59% improvement, while the lowest was observed with Cys-GQD NPs foliar application that was lessened by 21.18% over to control seedlings. DHA was meaningfully impacted by water deficit stress. In apricot seedlings, the DHA content diminished in severe drought stress and enhanced in 100% FC. The maximum DHA content was obtained in 100% FC with Cys-GQD NPs foliar application, which was improved up to 20.25%, and the lowest was observed in 25% FC without any foliar application, which was reduced by 104.17%. AsA/DHA activity in apricot plants was lowest in 100% FC with Cys-GQD NPs foliar application by 45.63% reduction but boosted up to 210.918% under 25% FC without any foliar application (Table 5).

Cys-GQDS nanocomposite × 25% FC treatment recorded a higher TAA (Table 4). In contrast, the lowest data related to this was obtained in the control apricot seedlings. Plants foliar sprayed with Cys-GQDs NPs nanocomposite at 25% FC had 16.51% higher TAA than the seedlings with no foliar spraying (Fig. 8 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pearson's correlations and biplot of principal component analysis (PCA) of cys-gqdnps-treated apricot seedlings grown under drought stress
ce:section.ce:para: The heat map of Pearson's correlation brought out the two clusters of the evaluated traits of apricot seedlings, which were treated with Cys, GQD, and Cys-GQD NPs in water deficit conditions. The first cluster was chlorophyll a and b, MSI, DHA, carbohydrate, carotenoids, leaf fresh weight, and RWC, which had a meaningful positive correlation with each other. The second cluster was APX, protein, flavonoids, TAA, PPO, phenolics content, SOD, GPX, EL, H2O2, AsA, AsA/DHA, MDA, and proline, and these characteristics positively correlated with each other while negatively correlated with the first cluster (Fig. 9 a).

The evaluated traits along with the applied treatments were exposed to the principal component and biplot analysis. The findings revealed that the PCA1 interpreted 83.1% of the observed variance, and the PCA2 led to 10.9% of the observed total variance among the evaluated traits. The biplot analysis showed that chlorophyll a and b, MSI, DHA, carbohydrate, carotenoids, leaf fresh weight, and RWC were considerably associated with the treatments of GQD NPs and Cys-GQD NPs with no and moderate water deficit. While SOD, phenolics content, GPX, protein, APX, TAA, and PPO were correlated to the foliar application of GQD and Cys-GQD NPs under severe water deficits. Finally, the traits of MDA, EL, H2O2, AsA, AsA/DHA, and proline were correlated with the treatments of foliar application of distilled water in moderate and severe water deficit, as well as Cys NPs foliar spraying in severe drought stress (Fig. 9b).

The cluster biplot analysis revealed three groups of treatments, which the first group included the foliar application of DW, Cys, GQDs, and Cys-GQDs NPs under normal irrigation. The second and third groups contained all of the foliar application under moderate and severe drought stress, respectively (Fig. 9c).

According to the current research, when plants are exposed to drought stress, they experience a decrease in both their fresh and dry weights. Water shortage has decreased the lavender plants' weight and productivity due to a lack of nutrients caused by drought effects on nitrogen flow and phosphorus and potassium release (Kausar et al., 2023; Soltanbeigi et al., 2021). This decrease is caused by a reduction in turgor pressure within the cells, which is a consequence of insufficient water supply. The reduced turgor pressure inhibits cell division and enlargement, ultimately leading to a reduction in plant fresh and dry weight (Delfine et al., 2002). Applying Cys, GQD, and Cys-GQD NPs to plants experiencing stress has been found to have a significant positive impact on their fresh and dry weights. Cysteine foliar spray can improve plant growth due to its role as a source of nitrogen and sulfur. Cysteine is a crucial amino acid for plant metabolism and protein assimilation, increasing cell formation and fresh and dry matter derived from the plant. Cysteine present in the electron transport system can also increase the rate of photosynthetic metabolism, resulting in a higher amount of photoassimilate for plant growth (Jobe et al., 2019). Villagarcia et al. (2012) discovered that the surface charge of carbon nanomaterials may stimulate the expression of the water channel protein gene (LeAqp2) in plants, thereby enhancing plant development. The benefits of graphene oxide are attributed to its enhancement of Indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) production and concentration, along with its facilitation of root development and elongation in plants (Cheng et al., 2016). The enhancement of root and biomass development results from the mitigation of oxidative stress mediated by graphene oxide, as shown by the levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Ren et al., 2016). These findings indicate that cysteine and GQDs are advantageous for growth promotion, and this research discovered that their concurrent application significantly influenced the growth parameters of apricot under drought stress.

During drought stress, the biosynthesis of chlorophyll is decreased, which in turn causes a reduction in the activity of photosynthesis (Hidangmayum et al., 2019). In other words, drought stress severely impacted the photosynthetic system and hindered the production of important pigments like chlorophyll a, b, and carotenoids (Kausar et al., 2023). The photosynthetic efficiency was significantly reduced as a direct result of stomatal closure and the down-regulation of crucial genes in both the photosynthetic and Calvin cycle pathways (Pandey et al., 2022). It is a well-established fact that chlorophyll b is more sensitive to osmotic stress compared to chlorophyll a, thus making it more susceptible to damage under drought stress (Shin et al., 2021), which is evident in the present results. According to previous studies, the use of graphene oxide treatment improved rice and milk thistle photosynthetic pigment content in abiotic stress conditions (Safikhan et al., 2018). The observed increase in light absorption can be attributed to the stimulating effect of carbon-based NPs. These NPs can penetrate chloroplast membranes and enhance the size and number of chloroplasts, as suggested by González-García et al. (2019). It has been reported that Cys can enhance the Chl a and b content in oat plants that are exposed to drought stress (Perveen et al., 2019). This positive effect is likely due to its ability to improve the absorption of N in plants, which is crucial for Chl biosynthesis (Sadak et al., 2022). According to a recent report by Elkelish et al. (2021), plants that were subjected to water stress showed a significant decrease in their level of carotenoids compared to plants that were well-watered. This could be attributed to the role that carotenoids play in the production of ABA, a key signaling hormone that helps plants respond to drought stress (Parry and Horgan, 1991). It was found that the application of Cys advanced the amount of carotenoids produced under conditions of limited water availability. This positive effect could be attributed to the presence of carotenoids that facilitate the xanthophyll cycle, which helps to dissipate excess light energy and enhance photosynthetic capacity during drought stress (Elkelish et al., 2021). Conversely, prior research indicates that a thicker upper epidermis of leaves can mitigate water loss, decrease transpiration, and augment the water retention capacity of plants. Additionally, thicker palisade tissue possesses a greater capacity for water and chloroplast storage, thereby enhancing photosynthetic efficiency and improving drought tolerance (Guo SuJuan and Wu YanQi, 2018; Si-Nan et al., 2010).

These findings indicate that apricot seedlings subjected to drought stress had significantly reduced EL, RWC, and MSI. The higher production of hydrogen peroxide and MDA induced decreases in the cell membrane integrity of plants under drought stress, leading to increased EL (Abdelaal et al., 2020). The excessive production of ROS (H2O2) under drought conditions causes lipid oxidation and reduces the MSI, leading to injury in plant membranes and reduced growth (Ali et al., 2021). Overproduction of ROS occurs due to an excessive reduction in the electron transport chain of mitochondria and chloroplasts under water stress (Abdelaal et al., 2020). According to the study, stressed plants treated with GQDs NPs showed a significant boost in antioxidant enzyme activity, including SOD, POD, and APX. We also observed lower levels of H2O2 and MDA, indicating that the antioxidant defense mechanism of the plants remained operational during the stress conditions. This ability to adapt to stress conditions may be due to the antioxidant enzyme activity that can scavenge redundant reactive oxygen species and reduce oxidative stress damage. The present findings are consistent with previous studies by Wu et al. (2017), who found that stimulating some antioxidant enzyme activity can help reduce oxidative stress damage, while Zhao et al. (2022) reported that GO can enhance their activity and diminish MDA and H2O2 accumulation, thereby reducing oxidative stress damage in soybeans. The application of Cys has been suggested to have a protective effect in plants, lessening MDA and H2O2 while simultaneously boosting the antioxidant capacity. Additionally, it is considered an important precursor for glutathione synthesis (Zagorchev et al., 2013)

Plant resistance against various abiotic stresses can be induced by the ROS scavenging mechanism, which involves the use of enzymatic and non-enzymatic antioxidant components (Attia et al., 2020). This research aligns with the findings of Shirani Bidabadi and Sharifi (2021), which stated that exposure to drought stress significantly increased the total antioxidant activity (TAA) and ferric-reducing antioxidant power. To counter the effects of drought stress and eliminate ROS, such as H2O2, plants enhance their antioxidant defense system by augmenting the total phenolic content (TPC) and TAA (Shirani Bidabadi and Sharifi, 2021). The current findings are in line with previous reports, which showed that applying graphene quantum dots and amino acid NPs reduced MDA under stress and non-stress conditions (Feng et al., 2019; Ganjavi et al., 2021). NP application is highly effective in reducing oxidative stress and boosting the antioxidant system, including the activity of antioxidant enzymes. This result is consistent with the findings of Abbas et al. (2015), who observed that improved activities of enzyme antioxidants as well as protein content can enhance the abiotic stress tolerance and growth of offshoots of Phoenix dactylifera L. In addition, using Cys treatment on vegetative plants and seeds at all stages has significantly increased the activity of antioxidant enzymes. This indicates that Cys can reduce the generation of ROS caused by water stress and thus minimize oxidative damage. Cys's antioxidant properties might be responsible for reducing ROS (Perveen et al., 2019).

The current results revealed that flavonoids and total soluble phenolics showed a meaningful enhancement in the drought-stressed plants in comparison to the well-watered plants, as well as by the combined Cys, GQD, and Cys-GQD NPs treatments. Flavonoids are compounds found in high concentrations in the epidermis of leaves. The phenylpropanoid pathway initiates the production of flavonoids. Commonly, flavonoids act as signaling molecules and protect the plant from UV radiation, and they also regulate the biosynthesis of plant hormones such as auxin and cytokinin (Kurepa et al., 2018). During stress conditions, dihydroxy B-ring-substituted flavonoids actively scavenge ROS and protect the plants from oxidative damage (Agati et al., 2012; Shomali et al., 2022). Nanographene oxide was shown to affect the buildup of total phenolics under both standard circumstances and abiotic stress. (Golkar et al., 2021). Abiotic stress resulted in an elevation in total phenolic content in wheat leaves, with the highest concentration seen in the GQD treatment under stress circumstances (Haydar et al., 2023).

Plants have developed a robust mechanism to cope with abiotic stress and retain cell turgor pressure, and proline biosynthesis stands out as one of the most effective adaptive mechanisms (Semida et al., 2020). The Cur experiment clearly shows that proline content experienced a significant increase under drought stress conditions. It was also found that the highest proline accumulation was observed in 25% FC. Proline unequivocally possesses antioxidant capabilities that effectively lessen lipid peroxidation and actively maintain cellular homeostasis by safeguarding the redox balance (Urmi et al., 2023). A study found that drought conditions increased proline synthesis, but functionalized carbon nanoparticles decreased proline content. It's unclear whether this is due to decreased synthesis or increased breakdown. The presence of CNPs may reduce the need for proline-based osmoprotectants (Alluqmani and Alabdallah, 2023). In soybeans previously exposed to graphene oxide, dryness did not seem to exacerbate osmotic stress, as proline levels remained unchanged. Consequently, pre-exposure to graphene oxide seems to have triggered biochemical alterations that pre-adapted roots to the osmotic effects of drought, resulting in reduced cellular adaptations when subsequently exposed to drought conditions. (Zhao et al., 2022). Also, external amino acids increase proline accumulation, which scavenges free radicals more efficiently than antioxidants. This buffers cellular redox potential during cabbage drought stress (Ashraf and Foolad, 2007). It is worth noting that applying exogenous amino acids results in a rapid increase in proline accumulation, leading to proline-protein interaction that effectively mitigates protein denaturation (Güneş et al., 1996). The accumulation of protein is essential for maintaining osmotic balance and membrane stability under stress (Qaseem et al., 2019).

AsA is a low molecular weight, non-enzymatic substance that scavenges ROS through the AsA-GSH cycle. The AsA system consists of AsA, MDHA, and DHA. APX oxidizes AsA to MDHA, which then converts to DHA during H2O2 removal. MDHAR reduces MDHA to AsA, and DHAR regenerates AsA from DHA. Therefore, the AsA/DHA ratio can indicate cell oxidation and reduction states (Jiang et al., 2022). The concentrations of AsA fluctuate in response to changes in the external environment, particularly under abiotic stress (Shan et al., 2020). The AsA are crucial non-enzymatic antioxidants in reducing oxidative stress and improving plant drought tolerance. It was reported that cassava seedling genotypes exhibited a significant increase in the contents of AsA under water-deficient conditions (Zhu et al., 2020). A study showed that spraying graphene NPs increased defense compounds and antioxidant enzymes. The growth-stimulating effect of GQDs NPs may be associated with their antioxidative properties, such as de novo synthesis of ascorbic acid and stimulation of ascorbate peroxidase (APX) activity. GQDs NPs enhance chloroplasts' antioxidant capacity by neutralizing ROS, thereby protecting them from aging (Akram et al., 2017). The application of GQDs NPs treatment in plants improved the ascorbate-glutathione pool, such as GSH, a low molecular weight antioxidant that plays a crucial role in maintaining the optimal redox status of cells (Kandziora-Ciupa et al., 2016). Consequently, the ascorbate-glutathione pool is subject to rigorous regulation by GQDs NPs, which work alongside CAT and APX to manage dangerous H2O2 levels. Furthermore, GPXs are thought to regulate cellular redox homeostasis by adjusting the thiol-disulfide balance (Bela et al., 2015).

The findings demonstrated the detrimental effects of drought stress on apricots' development and physiological characteristics. Focal spraying with cysteine-graphene quantum dots (GQDs-Cys) nanoparticles, on the other hand, resulted in an improvement in the physiological responses of the plant. With increasing levels of drought stress, the amount of photosynthetic pigments in apricot plants of the 'Asgarabad' variety declined. On the other hand, the amount of osmolytes, including proline, total soluble proteins, and antioxidant enzymes, increased. In both stressed and non-stressed settings, the application of GQDs-Cys nanoparticles resulted in a favorable impact on the augmentation of antioxidant defense levels and the improvement of the characteristics of the apricot plant that were currently under investigation. It can be concluded that this novel nanomaterial is a creative strategy that has the potential to be effectively used in apricot trees to increase their growth responses and yield potential when they are under the influence of drought stress. In conclusion, the findings of the current experiment will be encouraging for the development of apricot cultivation under circumstances of drought stress. However, these findings will need to be re-examined before being applied to the other commercial varieties and the related species.

Mohammad Ali Aazami: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Fatemeh Bahrevar: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Farzad Rasouli: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Mohammad Bagher Hassanpouraghdam: Writing – review & editing. Leila Khoshmaram: Writing – review & editing, Methodology.
